Overview Conditions Bonnet Experience In Build Pitch
A Mixed Reality Prototype

In the Wake of Vision

An immersive simulation of age-related macular degeneration and Charles Bonnet Syndrome — built for intergenerational understanding.

Format Mixed Reality / 360 Video
Duration ≈ 18 min
Status Prototype, 2026
Audience Family · Care · Education
Scroll
§ 01 · Premise

Two hundred and fifty years before virtual reality existed, a deaf Swiss philosopher watched his grandfather describe visions of buildings and birds that nobody else could see. Charles Bonnet was the first to record what we now call CBS — visual hallucinations born not of madness, but of a brain compensating for the silence of failing eyes.

In the Wake of Vision is a mixed-reality experience that places a sighted viewer briefly inside two of the most common, and most misunderstood, visual conditions of ageing: macular degeneration and Charles Bonnet Syndrome. It is built to be experienced together — grandchild beside grandparent, student beside elder — so that what is usually invisible becomes, for eighteen minutes, shared.

§ 02The Conditions

Two ways the world
quietly disappears.

01 · Age-related Macular Degeneration

The Central Blank

AMD erodes the macula — the small central patch of retina that delivers sharp, detailed sight. Faces become unreadable. The middle of every page goes missing. Peripheral vision remains, but the world the eye was built to focus on is the part that disappears.

It is the leading cause of vision loss in adults over sixty in the developed world. Most people in someone's family will, eventually, know somebody who has it.

Onset50+ yrs
FormDry · Wet
UK prev.~600 000
02 · Charles Bonnet Syndrome

The Hallucinated Guest

When the eye sends less, the visual cortex begins to invent. CBS is the experience of seeing vivid, often beautiful, sometimes uncanny things that aren't there — faces, patterns, animals, figures in costume — superimposed on a real visual field that is steadily withdrawing.

People with CBS are not psychotic. They know the visions are not real. But because the syndrome is so rarely explained — even to those living with it — many spend years assuming, in silence, that they are losing their minds.

LucidityPreserved
TriggerSensory loss
DiagnosedRarely
§ 03The Naming

A Mind That Did Not
Stop seeing.

Bonnet was a naturalist, philosopher and observer of insects, born in Geneva and deaf from about the age of seven. In later life his own eyesight began to fail. When his grandfather, Charles Lullin, began describing elaborate, peopled scenes that nobody else could see, Bonnet did not call them illness. He called them evidence.

He treated hallucinations as proof of something deeper: that the mind was an active generator of images, not a passive screen for the world. Long before neuroscience, he proposed that perception was a collaboration between sensation and memory — and that even as the senses fade, the inner world continues to compose.

Geneva, Switzerland
Lived and worked in Geneva. Deaf from early childhood. Sight deteriorated significantly in adulthood.
Mind as Generator
Argued the mind actively creates imagery, rather than passively receiving sensation.
Persistence of the Image
Maintained that internal ideas remain vivid even when external sensory input is weak or absent.
Hallucination as Compensation
Re-framed hallucinations in his grandfather as evidence of the mind responding to sensory loss — not as a sign of illness.
Mind–Body Parallelism
An early proposer of the link between mental imagery and physical processes in the brain.
§ 04The Experience

Eighteen minutes,
five stages.

01
360° Passive · 4 min

The Iris

The viewer falls into a slow, breathing iris. A narrator introduces ageing, sight and Bonnet himself. The composite portrait resolves; the historical anchor is set.

02
VR Interactive · 3 min

The Marbles

Darkness. A jar of luminous marbles appears. The viewer is invited to pick one up and hold it to their eyes — each marble triggers a different visual condition.

03
Mixed Reality · 3 min

The Central Blank

The viewer looks at the real room around them — but a black scotoma sits in the centre of their gaze. They are asked to read a physical page on the table in front of them, and to try.

04
Mixed Reality · 4 min

The Visitor

A figure that isn't there walks across the room. The hallucination drifts with the gaze, refuses to anchor. The viewer experiences, briefly, what their grandparent might never describe aloud.

05
Together · 4 min

The Conversation

Headset off. A guided prompt invites the elder and the younger viewer to talk: what they saw, what was familiar, what was wholly new. The experience ends not in the device, but between people.

§ 05In Build

The prototype is live
and being tested.

Documentation from current development sessions on Meta Quest. The scenes below are working passes — not final visuals — captured during iteration on hand-tracking, hallucination behaviour and the marble interaction.

Note · The VR captures shown here are first-pass development frames from a working prototype. Final scenes will use bespoke environmental modelling, period-accurate library imagery, and tuned hallucination behaviour informed by the clinical literature on CBS hallucination movement (see references).
§ 06For Intergenerational Settings

Empathy is not a
simulation. It is a seat
beside someone.

"When my grandmother told me she was seeing people in her kitchen, I thought she was losing her mind. Nobody had ever told either of us what was happening."

In the Wake of Vision is designed to be hosted in places where generations already meet — care homes, family homes, community libraries, schools, ophthalmology waiting rooms. It is not a clinical tool. It is a conversation prompt with eighteen minutes of weight behind it.

The closing stage — the conversation — is structured but unhurried. The viewer who has just experienced a scotoma sits with the person who has lived with one for years. The questions are simple, the silences are allowed, and the goal is recognition, not diagnosis.

Care Settings

Designed for use in residential care, day-care and dementia-friendly programmes. Single-headset, single-viewer cycles with rest intervals.

Family Use

Built for grandchildren, adult children and grandparents to share. A short version (≈10 min) is available for younger viewers and shorter attention windows.

Clinical Pathways

An optional briefing pack supports waiting-room or post-diagnosis use, co-developed with ophthalmology and low-vision specialists.

Programme it.
see what happens.

We are seeking gallery, museum and institutional partners for a 2026 limited-run installation.

References
& Sources

Clinical & historical literature underpinning the simulation.

  1. ffytche, D. H. (2005). Visual hallucinations and the Charles Bonnet syndrome. Current Psychiatry Reports, 7(3), 168–179.
  2. ffytche, D. H., & Howard, R. J. (1999). The perceptual consequences of visual loss: 'Positive' pathologies of vision. Brain, 122(7), 1247–1260.
  3. Menon, G. J. (2005). Complex visual hallucinations in the visually impaired: A structured history-taking approach. Archives of Ophthalmology, 123(3), 349–355.
  4. Teunisse, R. J., Zitman, F. G., & Cruysberg, J. R. M. (1996). Visual hallucinations in psychologically normal people: Charles Bonnet's syndrome. The Lancet, 347(9004), 794–797.
  5. Vukicevic, M., & Fitzmaurice, K. (2008). Butterflies and black lacy patterns: The prevalence and characteristics of Charles Bonnet hallucinations in an Australian population. Clinical & Experimental Ophthalmology, 36(7), 659–665.
  6. Cox, T. M., ffytche, D. H., Cooke, C. A., & Howard, R. J. (2003). Charles Bonnet syndrome: A systematic review of diagnostic criteria and treatment. Acta Psychiatrica Scandinavica, 107(6), 403–414.
  7. Jackson, M. L., & Ferencz, J. (2009). Charles Bonnet syndrome: Visual hallucinations in older adults. Journal of the American Geriatrics Society, 57(6), 1118–1120.
